title: Unit 04 Pretest
description: Diagnostic pretest for Media Literacy & Misinformation.

questions.prompt: What is the main goal of media literacy?

choices.text: To create media content professionally
choices.feedback: Media literacy focuses on understanding and evaluating media, not just creating it.

choices.text: To understand how media is created and evaluate its messages
choices.feedback: Correct. Media literacy involves understanding how media works and analyzing its messages.

choices.text: To increase the number of posts shared online
choices.feedback: Sharing more content is not the goal of media literacy.

choices.text: To avoid all forms of digital media
choices.feedback: Media literacy helps users engage critically, not avoid media entirely.

questions.prompt: Which example best shows bias in a news source?

choices.text: Providing multiple viewpoints with supporting evidence
choices.feedback: Multiple viewpoints typically reduce bias rather than show it.

choices.text: Using emotionally charged language to influence the reader’s opinion
choices.feedback: Correct. Loaded language is a key indicator of bias.

choices.text: Including verified data and statistics
choices.feedback: Verified data supports credibility, not bias.

choices.text: Citing reliable expert sources
choices.feedback: Reliable sources strengthen credibility rather than indicate bias.

questions.prompt: Misinformation and disinformation both involve false information, but disinformation is intentionally misleading.

choices.text: True
choices.feedback: Correct. Disinformation is intentionally false, while misinformation may be shared without intent to deceive.

choices.text: False
choices.feedback: There is an important difference: intent separates misinformation from disinformation.

questions.prompt: How can algorithms influence the information people see online?

choices.text: They show random content without patterns
choices.feedback: Algorithms are not random; they are designed to prioritize certain content.

choices.text: They organize content based only on time posted
choices.feedback: Algorithms consider many factors beyond timing.

choices.text: They prioritize content based on user behavior and preferences
choices.feedback: Correct. Algorithms shape what users see based on interactions and interests.

choices.text: They block all content that is repeated
choices.feedback: Algorithms do not block repeated content by default.

questions.prompt: Which action is most effective when fact-checking information online?

choices.text: Checking multiple credible sources to confirm the information
choices.feedback: Correct. Verifying information across reliable sources is a key fact-checking strategy.

choices.text: Trusting the first result in a search
choices.feedback: The first result is not always the most reliable.

choices.text: Relying on how many people liked the post
choices.feedback: Popularity does not equal accuracy.

choices.text: Assuming it is correct if it looks professional
choices.feedback: Professional appearance does not guarantee accuracy.

questions.prompt: A student sees a viral post making a strong claim without sources. What is the best next step?

choices.text: Share it quickly before it disappears
choices.feedback: Sharing unverified claims spreads misinformation.

choices.text: Ignore it without thinking further
choices.feedback: Ignoring does not build evaluation skills.

choices.text: Verify the claim using trusted sources before reacting
choices.feedback: Correct. Verification is the responsible response to questionable content.

choices.text: Assume it is true because it is popular
choices.feedback: Popularity does not determine accuracy.

questions.prompt: A headline designed to make people feel strong emotions may be a sign of unreliable information.

choices.text: True
choices.feedback: Correct. Emotional manipulation is a common feature of misleading content.

choices.text: False
choices.feedback: Strong emotional language can signal bias or manipulation.

questions.prompt: Which question best supports evaluating a media message?

choices.text: Does this match what I already believe?
choices.feedback: Personal bias can interfere with accurate evaluation.

choices.text: Who created this and what is their purpose?
choices.feedback: Correct. Identifying source and purpose is key to media analysis.

choices.text: How many people shared it?
choices.feedback: Popularity does not determine credibility.

choices.text: Is it easy to understand?
choices.feedback: Clarity does not guarantee accuracy.
